title: Hepcidin secreted by hepatocytes
vignette: A 41-year-old woman with a long history of heavy menstrual bleeding comes to the office for follow-up. Two weeks ago, the patient was diagnosed with iron deficiency anemia and was started on oral ferrous sulfate. Today, vital signs and physical examination are unremarkable. Laboratory evaluation shows an appropriate response to the medication with an increase in reticulocyte production. She is instructed to continue iron supplementation. The woman is concerned about iron buildup and damage to vital organs if she continues to take the medication. She is reassured that iron homeostasis is closely regulated; as the body's iron stores increase, a hormone is released and functions in reducing the amount of dietary iron absorbed from the intestines. Which of the following cells is most likely responsible for secreting this hormone?

clues.note: Frames the scenario around iron handling; her concern about iron overload prompts the explanation of the regulatory hormone. The diagnosis itself is background — the hormone identity is the target.

clues.text: a hormone is released and functions in reducing the amount of dietary iron absorbed
clues.note: This description IS hepcidin: when iron stores rise, hepcidin increases, degrades ferroportin, and reduces dietary iron absorption and macrophage iron release. Hepcidin is the master negative regulator of iron.

whatMatters: The described hormone — released when iron stores rise to cut intestinal iron absorption — is hepcidin. The only thing being asked is WHERE hepcidin is made: hepatocytes (hepatic parenchymal cells). Everything about her anemia and treatment is set dressing.
strategy: When a stem describes a hormone by function instead of naming it, identify the hormone first (negative iron regulator = hepcidin), then answer the asked detail (site of synthesis = liver/hepatocytes). Hepcidin is an acute-phase reactant made by the liver.

eliminations.label: marrow macrophages — TARGET of hepcidin, not the source
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: reticuloendothelial macrophages store iron from senescent RBCs and release it via ferroportin. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: they are ACTED ON by hepcidin (their ferroportin is degraded), not the cells that secrete it. ✅ Correct if: asked which cells store/recycle iron and are blocked by hepcidin.

eliminations.label: marrow stem cells — make blood cells, not hepcidin
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: hematopoietic stem cells generate erythroid and myeloid lineages. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: they don't secrete hepcidin; iron regulation by hepcidin is a hepatic function. ✅ Correct if: the question concerned erythropoiesis or a marrow-derived growth factor.

eliminations.label: intestinal epithelium — TARGET (absorption site), not source
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: duodenal enterocytes absorb dietary iron and export it via ferroportin. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: hepcidin acts ON enterocyte ferroportin to REDUCE absorption — these cells are the target, not the secretor. ✅ Correct if: asked where dietary iron is absorbed or where ferroportin is downregulated to limit uptake.

eliminations.label: renal tubular cells — that's erythropoietin (your pick)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: renal peritubular interstitial cells secrete erythropoietin in response to hypoxia. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: the kidney makes EPO, not hepcidin; you likely mixed up the two iron/erythropoiesis hormones. ✅ Correct if: the hormone described stimulated RBC production in response to hypoxia (EPO).

reasoning: Hormone that rises with iron stores and cuts intestinal iron absorption = hepcidin
Hepcidin degrades ferroportin on enterocytes and macrophages
Hepcidin is synthesized by the liver
Secreting cell = hepatic parenchymal cells (hepatocytes) → answer C
mechanism: Hepcidin is a peptide hormone made by hepatocytes; its production rises with high iron stores and with inflammation (it's a positive acute-phase reactant). Hepcidin binds ferroportin on duodenal enterocytes and reticuloendothelial macrophages, causing its internalization and degradation, which reduces dietary iron absorption and iron release from stores. High hepcidin therefore lowers serum iron — the basis of anemia of chronic disease — while low hepcidin permits more iron entry.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Heme > iron regulation: hepcidin (made by liver, degrades ferroportin, raised by iron load and inflammation) and ferroportin/transferrin/ferritin. Contrast EPO (kidney). Cross-ref anemia of chronic disease.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Name the hormone from its function (negative iron regulator = hepcidin), then answer the asked detail (site = hepatocytes). Don't stop at identifying the hormone — return to the exact question.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on hepcidin, ferroportin, transferrin vs ferritin, hepatic parenchymal cell, erythropoietin? Tell me which and I'll explain.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Renal tubular cells bait you toward EPO, and enterocytes/macrophages are hepcidin's TARGETS. Separate the source (liver) from the targets (gut/macrophage) and from the other hormone (kidney/EPO).

difficulty.label: 🖼️ Imaging / diagram
difficulty.message: The reference is the hepcidin/iron-regulation schematic (liver hepcidin → blocks ferroportin on enterocytes and macrophages). Use it to see source vs target cells at a glance.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Tell me what happened (timing, second-guessing, careless click) and I'll suggest a fix for future questions.

keywords.front: Hormone that lowers iron absorption when stores are high

keywords.back: ↓ serum iron → anemia of chronic disease

firstAid.detail: Heme > iron metabolism: hepcidin (hepatocyte-derived, degrades ferroportin), ferroportin, transferrin, ferritin; regulation by iron stores and inflammation.
firstAid.location: Heme > iron metabolism: hepcidin (hepatocyte-derived, degrades ferroportin), ferroportin, transferrin, ferritin; regulation by iron stores and inflammation.

firstAid.detail: Heme > microcytic anemias: iron deficiency (low ferritin, high TIBC), reticulocyte response to therapy.
firstAid.location: Heme > microcytic anemias: iron deficiency (low ferritin, high TIBC), reticulocyte response to therapy.

firstAid.detail: Renal/Heme > EPO from kidney (hypoxia); enterocyte iron absorption; macrophage iron recycling — to distinguish source vs target of hepcidin.
firstAid.location: Renal/Heme > EPO from kidney (hypoxia); enterocyte iron absorption; macrophage iron recycling — to distinguish source vs target of hepcidin.

memoryHook: HEPcidin comes from the HEPatocyte; it locks the iron 'door' (ferroportin) on gut and macrophages. EPO is the kidney's hormone — don't swap them.
